coredata.dc:description: Development of materials exhibiting both multicolor room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) and thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF), with tunable afterglow characteristics, is still a challenge. In this work, a surface engineering method is employed to obtain carbon dots (CDs) with dual multicolor RTP and TADF. These CDs achieve maximum quantum yield of 39.97 % and lifetime of 1.82 s, with visible luminescence persisting up to 15 s. Additionally, the CDs-IV composites demonstrate dual emissions, featuring yellow RTP and red TADF, with approximately 250 % increase in intensity at 640 nm, with long lifetime up to 115 ms as temperature rises from 313 to 358 K. This enhancement is achieved by integrating seed CDs into the ionic crystal matrix, which enhances reverse intersystem crossing by increasing spin-orbit coupling between singlet and triplet states while reducing non-radiative decay, resulting in a thermally enhanced TADF response. The robust interaction within the crystal network effectively stabilizes the triplet state, ensuring the material's stability in water and resistance to harsh conditions, such as strong acids and oxidants.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A schematic illustrating the design strategy and synthesis of the CDs-I, CDs-II, CDs-III, and CDs-IV.
(a–b) TEM, HR-TEM images, and size distribution of CDs-II, (c) CDs-III (d) CDs-IV.
(a–c) XRD patterns, (d–f) FT-IR, and (g–i) XPS survey spectra of CDs-II, CDs-III, and CDs-IV.

(a) Images of CDs-II, CDs-III, and CDs-IV composites powder post-excitation (365 nm). (b–d) PL spectrum (black) and RTP spectrum (red) of CDs-II, CDs-II, CDs-III, and CDs-IV composites. (e–g) RTP decay lifetime spectra of CDs-II, CDs-III, and CDs-IV composites were monitored at various peaks under 365 nm UV light. (h) Time-resolved fluorescence (i) CIE coordinates. (j) Quantum yields a comparison of all samples.

(a) Images of CDs-IV powder at 373 K with delay time. (b–d) TADF emission spectra of CDs-II, CDs-III and CDs-IV at different temperatures. (e) Temperature-dependent decay lifetime of CDs-IV (f) CIE chromaticity diagram. (g) Comparison of lifetimes. (h) TGA of CDs-IV. (i) Optical photographs of (i) CDs-II, (ii) CDs-III, and (iii) CDs-IV dispersed in various oxidizing agents and solvents.
(a) Conditions necessary for the mechanisms of RTP and TADF. (b) The suggested approach for realizing TADF with an enhanced duration.

Application of CDs composites. a) Encryption and anticounterfeiting of numbers. b) QR code multi-encryption. (c) Anticounterfeiting of graphics using CDs composite materials with different lifetimes. (d) Multicolor afterglow demonstration of light-emitting diodes. e) Afterglow photographs of CDs-IV composites at different temperatures and corresponding CIE coordinates.

head.ce:title: Carbon dot composites of multicolor dual-mode phosphorescence and thermally enhanced delayed emission

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Development of materials exhibiting both multicolor room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) and thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF), with tunable afterglow characteristics, is still a challenge. In this work, a surface engineering method is employed to obtain carbon dots (CDs) with dual multicolor RTP and TADF. These CDs achieve maximum quantum yield of 39.97 % and lifetime of 1.82 s, with visible luminescence persisting up to 15 s. Additionally, the CDs-IV composites demonstrate dual emissions, featuring yellow RTP and red TADF, with approximately 250 % increase in intensity at 640 nm, with long lifetime up to 115 ms as temperature rises from 313 to 358 K. This enhancement is achieved by integrating seed CDs into the ionic crystal matrix, which enhances reverse intersystem crossing by increasing spin-orbit coupling between singlet and triplet states while reducing non-radiative decay, resulting in a thermally enhanced TADF response. The robust interaction within the crystal network effectively stabilizes the triplet state, ensuring the material's stability in water and resistance to harsh conditions, such as strong acids and oxidants.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Afterglow luminescence refers to the prolonged emission of light from materials after removal of the excitation source and includes forms such as phosphorescence, long-persistent luminescence, or delayed fluorescence [1–3]. These materials are often distinguished by their high signal-to-noise ratios, long luminescence decay times, and large Stokes shifts [4,5]. Their unique properties make them suitable for a wide range of applications, such as bioimaging, sensing, information encryption, anticounterfeiting, and light-emitting diodes (LEDs) [6–8]. Multicolor RTP materials have gained significant attention for their potential in multicolor displays and multichannel information encryption due to their vibrant and tunable color properties [9].

Materials with adjustable RTP can also transform invisible external stimuli, such as metal ions or gases, into visible light signals, providing an effective platform for the visual detection and perception of various external factors [10]. While traditional RTP materials are often inorganic or metal-organic complexes containing costly and toxic noble metals, organic RTP materials offer a less expensive and less toxic alternative [11]. However, these organic materials typically suffer from short lifetimes, complex synthesis methods, and limited stability [12]. CDs, a type of luminescent carbon nanomaterial, have gained significant attention owing to their outstanding optical properties, environmentally friendly synthesis, and low toxicity [13–15]. These materials are cost-effective and can be produced through simple processes. Structurally, CDs feature hybrid core-shell arrangement, with carbon core composed of sp2-hybridized carbon atoms and surface rich in functional groups [16,17]. Some CDs-based afterglow materials demonstrate strong resistance to harsh conditions, making them suitable for practical applications [18]. Several strategies can be employed to design multicolor RTP materials, adjusting carbonization levels, including secondary carbonization, to control emission wavelengths; manipulating aggregation to affect triplet states and energy levels, thus enabling multicolor luminescence; incorporating multiple luminescent centre's in CD-matrix composites to achieve color variation dependent on excitation or time and utilizing Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), with CDs serving as energy donors and fluorescent dyes as acceptors, to fine-tune afterglow colors and lifetimes [19–23]. However, raising the reaction temperature or increasing the CDs concentration often leads to significant decrease in afterglow lifetime, while energy transfer methods tend to suffer from energy loss, limiting their practical applications [24,25].

TADF is a distinct type of radioluminescence, where an exciton moves from the triplet (T1) state to the singlet (S1) state via RISC, followed by radiative transition from the S1 state to the ground state (S0) [7]. Efficient TADF requires two main conditions, a rapid RISC rate from the triplet to singlet states to counteract non-radiative decay and quenching at the T1 state, and stabilization of excited triplet species to reduce non-radiative relaxation [26,27]. Balancing TADF and RTP remains significant challenge since they are competing processes [28]. While most studies on CDs mainly focus on either RTP or TADF, materials exhibiting both properties are rare. Integrating both TADF and RTP within one material is complex [5,29]. Typically, TADF materials use hydrogen bonding to stabilize triplet excitons, but the relatively weak strength of these bonds limits their effectiveness in suppressing non-radiative recombination [30]. As a result, these materials often lack thermal enhancement at higher temperatures, where non-radiative processes dominate. Compared to weak hydrogen bonds, strong covalent bonds between CDs and matrices provide more effective solution for stabilizing and rigidifying triplet emission species [18]. However, achieving key properties such as thermally enhanced TADF emission and extended lifetimes in CDs materials remains challenging. Additionally, the non-radiative decay and quenching rates of triplet states tend to increase, especially when the triplet state energy levels are reduced in accordance with the energy gap law, hindering long-wavelength emissions. As a result, most reported CD-based TADF emissions are limited to blue or green regions, and only a few CDs with emissions exceeding 600 nm have been observed thus far (Table S1) [31]. Therefore, the development of new CDs-based TADF materials with enhanced thermal emission, longer lifetimes, and extended wavelengths is crucial to broaden their potential multifunctional applications.

In this work, we designed highly stable dual-mode luminescent CDs composites exhibiting both RTP and TADF through a surface modification approach by incorporating urea, (3-aminopropyl)triethoxysilane, and potassium hydroxide (KOH) into seed CDs. The resulting multicolor CDs showcased broad RTP emissions peaks at 478, 554, and 622 nm, with maximum lifetimes reaching 1.82 s and quantum yields of up to 39.97 %. Notably, the CDs-IV demonstrated thermally enhanced luminescence, showing a 2.8-fold increase in intensity at 640 nm as the temperature rose from 313 to 358 K. Detailed analyses revealed that the incorporation of KOH significantly enhanced ionization of surface carboxyl groups, forming ionic bonds and a rigid network that suppresses non-radiative decay. Moreover, these composites showed exceptional resistance to thermal degradation, oxidation, and polar solvents, rendering them suitable for multicolor long-lasting light illumination, and, time-temperature-responsive encryption applications.

3-Aminopropyl triethoxysilane (APTES) and urea (99.5 %), potassium hydroxide (KOH), sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and ethanol were sourced from Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. walnut leaves were picked from the Central South University Garden. All the reagents can be used directly without further purification. The ultrapure (Milli-Q) water used in the experiment was purified by the SZ-93A pure water system.

2-Gram walnut leaves were ground using a mortar and further dissolved in 10 mL of ethanol solution, ultrasonicated for 15 min, and centrifuged at 10000 r min−1 for 8 min to purify the CDs, resulting in the liquid supernatant was collected to obtain the seed CDs ethanol solution.

CDs-II was fabricated with 300 g urea and 135 uL APTES with 5 mL deionized water into a 20 mL glass container, which was immersed in an oil bath at 170 °C for 90 min, allowing samples to cooled down to ambient temperature to obtain CDs-II.

CDs-III was prepared using 2 mL seed-CDs, 300 mg urea, and 135 uL APTES with 10 mL ethanol were mixed in a 20 mL glass container, which was subjected to heating in an oil bath at 170 °C for 90 min, allowing samples to cooled down to ambient temperature to obtain CDs-III.

CDs-IV was prepared with 300 mg urea, 25 mg KOH, 135 uL APTES with 10 mL ethanol and were mixed in a 20 mL glass container, which was placed in an oil bath at 170 °C for a duration of 90 min, allowing samples to cooled down to ambient temperature to obtain CDs-IV. Additional two composites were prepared under identical conditions, replacing KOH with NaOH and NH3·H2O resulting in (CDs-IV/NaOH) and (CDs-IV/NH3·H2O) for comparison.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images were obtained using a Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN instrument. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed with an ESCALab 220i-XL electron spectrometer (VG Scientific). Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded on a Rigaku Ultima IV system utilizing Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5405 Å). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were acquired using a VERTEX-80 (Bruker, Germany). Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were measured with a TU-1900 (Metash, China). Photoluminescence (PL) measurements were conducted using an Edinburgh FS5 spectrophotometer. Afterglow spectra, decay time, and quantum yields were measured using an FLS1000 spectrofluorometer. Thermogravimetric analysis was performed using thermogravimetric Analyzers TGA 5500.

To achieve efficient RTP and TADF in CDs, two key criteria must be addressed. Firstly, stabilization of triplet excited states is crucial, which can be achieved through molecular engineering techniques such as urea treatment, which adds lone-pair electron-rich functional groups to the CD surface, thereby promoting ISC and reducing quenching rates. Additionally, utilizing an RTP-assisted energy transfer (ET) strategy has been shown to significantly prolong the lifetime of luminescent materials [32]. Therefore, by confining CDs within a rigid crystal network and leveraging the RTP-assisted ET strategy, it is possible to achieve efficient TADF with an extended lifetime [33]. Based on the outlined proposed framework, a surface modification approach was applied to attain efficient and prolonged multicolor RTP and TADF in carbon dot composites. The process began with the synthesis of seed CDs through an ultrasonication of walnut leaves using ethanol as the reactive solvent. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of the seed CDs, shown in Fig. S1, reveals that the CDs are monodispersed with an average size of approximately 3.5 nm. A detailed discussion about the spectroscopic properties of seed CDs is given in supporting information. Although the seed CDs did not exhibit afterglow in either their solution or solid state, as shown in Fig. S5, their structure holds significant promise for achieving red-delayed fluorescence. The process for enhancing this property involves a specific modulation technique outlined in Fig. 1 . In this method, 2 mL of seed CDs, 300 mg of urea, and 135 μL of APTES are first dissolved in 10 mL of ethanol. The mixture is then heated in an oil bath at 170 °C for 90 min, resulting in the formation of multicolor RTP CDs, specifically CDs@SiO2 (CDs-II), Seed CDs@SiO2 (CDs-III), Seed CDs@SiO2/KOH (CDs-IV) composite.

Subsequently, CDs-II, CDs-III, and CD-IV composites were used as prototypes for an in-depth investigation into the mechanism behind RTP and TADF. The morphologies of these CDs were examined using TEM as shown in Fig. 2 (a–d).

As shown in Fig. 2(a)(b) the CDs-II has been embedded in a matrix, and the HRTEM images further illustrate that these CDs exhibit a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which corresponds to the d-spacing of the graphene (100) planes [34]. The average particle size with diameters centred around 2.2 ± 0.5 nm Fig. 2(b)(inset). To investigate, the potential for TADF, the TEM and HRTEM are further analyzed before and after treatment of KOH for CDs-III and CDs-IV. As shown in Fig. 2(c)(d), reveal that both CDs-III and CDs-IV composites are embedded within a matrix with diameters centred around 4.2 ± 0.4 nm and 4.7 ± 0.3 nm, respectively. High-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images further illustrate that these CDs exhibit a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which corresponds to the d-spacing of the graphene (100) planes [35]. The seed CDs, which have an average size of 2.2 nm, show an increase in particle size in the CDs-III and CDs-IV samples. This increase in size is likely due to additional crosslinking reactions with urea during the synthesis process. The XRD pattern of CDs-II, and CDs-III shown in Fig. 3(a)(b), indicates the presence of an amorphous silica phase, as evidenced by the broad peak around 21.9°, confirming the CDs were successfully embedded into the SiO2 matrix [18,36]. In contrast, the XRD pattern of CD-IV displays not only the broad amorphous SiO2 peak but also prominent crystalline peaks associated with KCNO suggesting success embedded within KCNO crystals as shown in Fig. 3(c) [37]. The formation of KCNO likely occurs at high temperatures when KOH reacts with urea, which can be further supported by subsequent FT-IR analyses.

The FT-IR spectra of CDs-II, CDs-III, and CDs-IV, depicted in Fig. 3(d–f), reveal broad absorption bands in the range of approximately 2600 to 3600 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of methylene (–CH2), hydroxyl (–OH), and amino (−NH2) groups [38]. Additionally, peaks observed at 1712 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of carbonyl groups (C=O) [39]. The spectra also show 1101 cm−1 and 1026 cm−1 peaks for all samples, associated with the stretching vibrations of Si–O–Si and Si–O–C bonds indicating the formation of covalent bonds between the CDs and the SiO2 matrix during the reaction process [25,36]. Furthermore, within this matrix, hydrogen bonds can form between the hydroxyl and amino groups on the CD surfaces, which helps to minimize vibration-induced non-radiative transitions [40]. A comparison of the FT-IR spectra of CDs-II, CDs-III, and CDs-IV reveals two significant differences. First, the CDs-IV sample exhibits a strong absorption band at 2250 cm−1, which is attributed to the CN stretch in potassium cyanate (KCNO confirming that KCNO is present in the CDs-IV sample), likely formed during the reaction with KOH [37]. Secondly, an additional absorption band is observed at 1465 cm−1 in the CDs-IV spectrum, corresponding to the asymmetric stretching vibration of carboxylate groups (COO−). This suggests that ionic bonds may have formed between the CDs and the surrounding matrix, highlighting the presence of carboxylate groups that could interact with the CDs through ionic bonding. Moreover, the XPS spectra of CDs-II and CDs-III in Fig. 3(g–i) illustrate that CDs exhibit key elements C, N, O, and Si with binding energies of approximately 285.1 eV (C 1 s), 402.0 eV (N 1 s), 530.4 eV (O 1 s), and 103.3 eV (Si 2p) [36,41]. The XPS spectra of CDs-IV display additional peaks at 295.1 eV, corresponding to the K 2p binding energy (Fig. 3i). This indicates that the CDs are rich in nitrogen, oxygen, and silicon-containing functional groups. In Fig. S6(a–c), the XPS C 1 s spectra of high-resolution XPS spectra and fitting results of C 1 s of CDs-II, CDs-III, and CDs-IV four distinct peaks at binding energies of 284.0, 285.0, 288.0, and 289.0 eV [42]. The peak at 284.0 eV is assigned to SiC bonds, which suggests that some of the carbon atoms are chemically bonded to silicon within the silica matrix [36,43]. The slightly higher binding energy peak at 285 eV is attributed to CN. The peak at 288.0 eV represents CO bonds, indicating the presence of carbon atoms linked to oxygen, likely forming ether or alcohol groups. Finally, the peak at 289.7 eV is associated with O-C=O bonds, which are characteristic of carboxyl groups. In Fig. S7(a–c), the XPS N 1 s spectra of CDs-II, CDs-III, and CDs-IV are deconvoluted into three peaks at 398.1, 400.4, and 403.3 eV representing pyridinic N, Pyrrolic N, and oxidized NO [44,45]. Specifically, the XPS spectrum for O 1 s in CDs-II reveals' peaks at 531.7 eV and 532.4 eV, attributed to CO and C-O/Si-O bonds, respectively (Fig. S8) [46,47]. In Fig. S9(a–c), the high-resolution Si 2p spectrum has two distinct binding energies at approximately 102.9 and 101.5 eV. These peaks can be attributed to OSi and CSi bonds, respectively, indicating the presence of silicon atoms bonded to both oxygen and carbon [48]. In contrast, for CDs-IV additional peaks corresponding to K are observed at around 292.4 and 295.3 eV, confirming the formation of potassium ionic bonds (Fig. S10). Collectively, these results demonstrate the formation of multiple ionic bonds on the surface of CD-IV, these findings from XPS analysis are consistent with the FT-IR data, which are crucial for enhancing TADF emission in the CDs-IV.

As shown in Fig. S5, the seed CDs (CDs-I) do not produce any afterglow at room temperature. However, CDs-II, CDs-III, and CDs-IV exhibit bright and long-lasting luminescence in blue, green, and yellow, respectively, which persists for 12–15 s after the 365 nm excitation is turned off (Fig. 4a, Videos S1, S2, S3 supplementary information). The CIE coordinate diagram (Fig. 4i) reveals that the coordinates for CDs-II, CDs-III, and CDs-IV are (0.22, 0.32), (0.42, 0.52), and (0.43, 0.51), aligning with the visual observations. CDs-II displays blue PL with a peak at 400 nm when excited at 365 nm, and upon removal of the light source, the afterglow shifts to 478 nm as shown in Fig. 4b. The singlet-triplet energy gap ΔEST is determined using the formula:
(1)
ΔE ST
=
E S1
−
E T1
where ES1 represents the energy of the singlet excited state, and ET1 denotes the energy of the triplet excited state. In this study, a calculated ΔEST of 0.503 eV was observed, facilitating an efficient intersystem crossing (ISC) process. This efficient ISC mechanism is critical in sustaining the afterglow emission by promoting triplet state population and energy transfer [43]. The phosphorescence decay lifetime for this process is calculated to be 1.79 s, as shown in Fig. 4e. Similarly, CDs-III presents two PL peaks at 475 nm and 675 nm, with its delayed emission red shifting compared to its PL, resulting in two primary phosphorescence peaks at 554 nm and 675 nm (Fig. 4c). The ΔEST between the T1 and S1 states are calculated to be 0.318 eV and 0.016 eV, respectively, which enhances the triplet exciton population, contributing to prolonged phosphorescence. The phosphorescence lifetimes for the two peaks are calculated to be 1.01 s and 0.65 s, as illustrated in Fig. 4f. Furthermore, CD-IV shown in Fig. 4d, exhibits two PL peaks at 455 nm and 640 nm, with a notable redshift in the phosphorescence peaks to 585 nm and 650 nm. The phosphorescence lifetimes for these peaks are 0.98 s and 0.67 s, respectively, as presented in Fig. 4g. The calculated ΔEST values of 0.612 eV and 0.031 eV promote efficient ISC, enhancing triplet state population and resulting in a more sustained afterglow under ambient conditions [18]. The fluorescence decay lifetimes for CDs-II, CDs-III, and CDs-IV are 5.32 ns, 6.19 ns, and 4.73 ns, respectively (Fig. 4h). The total quantum yields (QYs) of CDs-I, CDs-II, CDs-III and CDs-IV were found to be 10.32 %, 39.97 %, 15.45 % and 29.13 % respectively (Fig. 4j). To further understand the long-lived afterglow mechanism of CDs-II, CDs-III, and CDs-IV, variable temperature tests were conducted for all samples recorded. As illustrated in Fig. 5(a) and Video S4 Supporting information, CDs-IV exhibits bright red TADF at 373 K, while both CDs-II and CDs-III do not display any TADF under the same conditions. Additionally, in CDs-II and CDs-III, the afterglow intensity markedly decreased with increasing temperature from 298 to 373 K as depicted in Fig. 5(b) and (c). The variable temperature spectra and lifetimes confirmed that the afterglow of these composites was typically phosphorescent. Between 343 and 373 K, CDs-III exhibits new emission peaks at approximately 520 and 670 nm, resulting in a noticeable shift in luminescence as shown in Fig. S16. These delayed emission bands are linked to RTP [49]. Although a small ΔEST is generally known to facilitate RISC from the triplet to singlet state, TADF was not observed in CDs-II and CDs-III, despite their relatively small ΔEST values [50]. This is similar to some organic compounds with ΔEST values under 0.2 eV, where TADF is absent due to slow RISC rates (less than 103 s−1) [51]. While a small ΔEST typically supports the RISC process, enabling the transition from T1 to S1 the lack of TADF in CDs-III, despite having a ΔEST of just 0.016 eV, is likely caused by rapid non-radiative decay and quenching of the triplet state [3,52]. The stronger delayed emission observed at 555 nm, combined with the human eye's reduced sensitivity to near-infrared (NIR) wavelengths around 700 nm, contributes to the direct perception of the yellow-green delayed emission from the material [31]. As illustrated in Fig. 5d, CDs-IV exhibits bright yellow delayed luminescence at room temperature. In contrast to its PL emission, the delayed emission shows prominent peaks at 585 and 640 nm, with additional shoulder peaks at 530 nm. The close alignment of the prompt and delayed emission peaks at 585 and 650 nm strongly suggests the occurrence of TADF in CDs-IV due to their minimal ΔEST [53]. Interestingly, as the temperature rises to 343 K, CDs-IV demonstrates a remarkable thermally enhanced emission, with the intensity at 640 nm increasing by 2.8 times (Fig. 5d). The decay lifetime afterglow of CDs-IV at different temperatures exhibited as a function of temperature (Fig. 5e). These findings suggest that the gradual enhancement in delayed emission with increasing temperature may be attributed to both TADF and RTP [54,55].

As shown in Fig. S5, the seed CDs (CDs-I) do not produce any afterglow at room temperature. However, CDs-II, CDs-III, and CDs-IV exhibit bright and long-lasting luminescence in blue, green, and yellow, respectively, which persists for 12–15 s after the 365 nm excitation is turned off (Fig. 4a, Videos S1, S2, S3 supplementary information). The CIE coordinate diagram (Fig. 4i) reveals that the coordinates for CDs-II, CDs-III, and CDs-IV are (0.22, 0.32), (0.42, 0.52), and (0.43, 0.51), aligning with the visual observations. As illustrated in Fig. 5d, CDs-IV exhibits bright yellow delayed luminescence at room temperature. In contrast to its PL emission, the delayed emission shows prominent peaks at 585 and 640 nm, with additional shoulder peaks at 530 nm. The close alignment of the prompt and delayed emission peaks at 585 and 650 nm strongly suggests the occurrence of TADF in CDs-IV due to their minimal ΔEST [53]. Interestingly, as the temperature rises to 343 K, CDs-IV demonstrates a remarkable thermally enhanced emission, with the intensity at 640 nm increasing by 2.8 times (Fig. 5d). The decay lifetime afterglow of CDs-IV at different temperatures exhibited as a function of temperature (Fig. 5e). These findings suggest that the gradual enhancement in delayed emission with increasing temperature may be attributed to both TADF and RTP [54,55].

At low temperatures, the absence of thermal assistance prevents the RISC process, allowing only emissions originating from triplet-excited states [56]. As shown in Fig. S17(a), the TADF spectrum of CDs-IV at 77 K closely resembles that of the RTP spectrum of CDs-III at room temperature. The emission peak at 678 nm, though weaker and shorter-lived, confirms its TADF origin, while the 518 nm emission, attributed to triplet states, indicates RTP, unaffected by the suppressed RISC process. Temperature-dependent decay lifetime measurements reveal a dominant yellow-green afterglow at 555 nm with a lifetime of up to 6 s, contrasted with the weaker emission at 678 nm with a lifetime of 0.1 s (Fig. S17(b)). At 77 K, the absence of sufficient thermal energy inhibits the RISC process leading to reduced delayed emission intensity and a shorter lifetime. This confirms that the lack of thermal activation restricts RISC, emphasizing the true TADF nature of CDs-IV. The prominence of the 555 nm emission, combined with the human eye's reduced sensitivity to near-infrared wavelengths, accounts for the observed yellow-green afterglow. CDs-IV displays a temperature-dependent delayed emission, shifting from yellow to orange and finally to bright red after the excitation source is removed Fig. S16. According to the CIE coordinate diagram (Fig. 5f), the emission coordinates gradually shift from (0.46, 0.51) to (0.56, 0.41), aligning with the visual changes observed. These observations indicate that CDs-IV is a bimodal afterglow material, where both RTP and TADF coexist. At room temperature, the material primarily exhibits phosphorescence, but as the temperature rises, TADF becomes more prominent. This behavior can be explained by the suppression of RISC at lower temperatures, causing the CDs-IV to act as a phosphorescent material. With increasing temperature, more triplet excitons transition into singlet states via RISC, leading to a gradual conversion of RTP into TADF. Further temperature increases result in thermal quenching, where energy is lost through non-radiative transitions, reducing luminescence intensity. Additionally, Fig. 5g highlights that most red TADF materials, as of August 2024, have average lifetimes shorter than 6.6 ms (Table S1) [57]. However, CD-IV stands out with a significantly longer lifetime of 115 ms at 640 nm, far exceeding previous reports [30,52,57].

The TGA curves for CDs-I to CDs-III, as presented in Fig. S11. CDs-I undergoes significant weight loss, around 80 %, at 170 °C. In contrast, CDs-II and CDs-III show a three-step thermal decomposition process. CDs-IV, as depicted in Fig. 5(h), displays an initial 5.10 % weight loss at 270.38 °C, likely due to the release of surface-bound groups [58]. A subsequent 15.48 % weight loss at 404.25 °C corresponds to the degradation of organic functional groups, and an additional 17.63 % loss at 514.77 °C indicates the decomposition of the carbon structure, confirming the CDs' thermal stability up to 500 °C. Since triplet excitons are prone to quenching by oxygen and water, the CDs were encapsulated in a urea matrix through various bonding methods to minimize non-radiative decay of the triplet excitons, effectively reducing afterglow quenching [25]. After being exposed to 365 nm UV light for 14 days, the afterglow intensities of the CDs-II, CDs-III, and CDs-IV composites emitting at 478, 554, and 640 nm remained above 90 % of their initial values, demonstrating that the urea matrix effectively enhanced their photostability as shown in Fig. S12 [59]. This highlights the CD's composites' excellent resistance to photobleaching. Even after 7 days of heating at 60 °C followed by cooling, their afterglow intensity remained above 90 % of the original Fig. S13. The long-term stability test over 2 months showed that the afterglow intensity of CDs-II to CDs-IV composites retained more than 80 % of their initial value. Additionally, these composites exhibited strong resistance to water quenching, as their afterglow remained visible after 30 min of ultrasonication and full dispersion in water. Under 365 nm UV excitation, the afterglow of CDs-II, CDs-III, and CDs-IV was visible to the naked eye for 8, 5, and 3 s, respectively, as shown in Fig. S14.

The CDs composites demonstrated remarkable durability in extreme environments, including exposure to strong acids, oxidizing agents, and a range of organic solvents. When dispersed in 30 wt% H₂O₂, 30 wt% HCl, and 98 wt% H₂SO₄, and subjected to 20 min of ultrasonication to ensure full dispersion, the afterglow intensity decreased slightly but remained visible to the naked eye (Fig. 5i). To further assess their stability, the CDs composites were tested in seven solvents with different polarities, where their afterglow showed minimal change, remaining stable in most cases (Figs. 5i, S15). To assess the effect of rigidity, we replaced KOH with NaOH under the same conditions. Stability tests for CDs-IV/NaOH showed a decreased afterglow intensity, particularly in water, hydrogen peroxide, and strong acids. The differences in afterglow properties between CDs-IV/NaOH and CDs-IV/KOH are attributed to the stronger interactions of potassium ions (K+) with precursor materials like APTES and urea [60]. The larger, more polarizable K+ ions enhance defect engineering, energy transfer, and multi-confinement within the matrix, resulting in superior afterglow intensity and better resistance to hydrolysis and oxidative quenching in KOH-based composites [61]. This stability is not just due to physical mixing but results from more intricate interactions [62]. The amino groups of urea chemically react with the carboxyl groups on the CDs' surface at elevated temperatures, forming strong crosslinking and chemical bonds. This ensures a durable attachment between urea and CDs. In addition to covalent and hydrogen bonding, CDs-II and CDs-III also rely on physical immobilization, while CDs-IV includes ionic bonding through the addition of KOH, enhancing their stability. This robust bonding allows the composites to perform well even in harsh conditions, making them suitable for a wide range of applications.

The phosphorescence mechanism of CD-II and CDs-III derived from APTES (3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane) can be explained by the dual role of APTES as both a carbon and nitrogen source, facilitating the formation of CDs. The hydrolysis of the silyl ether groups from APTES creates a cross-linked silica matrix surrounding the CDs, potentially forming a CD/silica monolith [36]. According to the FT-IR and XPS analysis, CD-II consists primarily of a carbon core (CC bonds) along with minor oxygen- and nitrogen-related surface groups. These surface groups contribute to (n, π*) transitions within the CDs, which are beneficial to promote the ISC from singlet to triplet excited states [2]. Although ISC from the singlet state to the triplet state endows phosphorescence, phosphorescence is still not observed from seed CDs due to the inefficient ISC process and the rapid rate of non-radiative deactivation as illustrated in Fig. 6a. However, after modification of the seed CDs with urea, additional functional groups, such as amine and carbonyl, rich in (n, π*) transitions, are introduced, which enhance the SOC and decrease the energy gap, which supports stronger phosphorescence properties by promoting more efficient ISC as observed in CDs-III (Fig. 1) [27]. To confirm the role of ionic interactions in TADF emission, CDs-IV composites were prepared using different hydroxides under identical conditions, replacing KOH with NaOH and NH3·H2O. As shown in Fig. S18, the NaOH-based composite (CDs-IV/NaOH) exhibited thermally enhanced TADF emission with distinct peaks at approximately 671 nm, similar to the KOH-based composite (CDs-IV/KOH), confirming that both hydroxides provide sufficient ionic bonding and rigidity to promote TADF. In contrast, the NH3·H2O-based composite (CDs-IV/NH3·H2O) exhibited only RTP features, indicating that in the absence of strong ionic bonding, as provided by KOH or NaOH, TADF emission does not occur. This highlights the critical role of ionic interactions in facilitating TADF. To further investigate the role of a rigid environment with strong ionic bonds in enhancing TADF, experiments were conducted using CDs-III without the addition of KOH. As the temperature increased from 298 K to 373 K in the CDs-III composites, the peak intensities associated with RTP at approximately 520 nm and 670 nm diminished. This temperature rise significantly accelerated the rate of non-radiative relaxation, depleting singlet excitons and consequently reducing the overall delayed emission intensity. As shown in Fig. 5c, no TADF emission was observed in these modified systems in the absence of KOH. This indicates that the combination of urea, APTES, and KOH in the CDs-IV system synergistically promotes the generation of TADF, which is absent without KOH. Therefore, the TADF emission can be proposed based on the following key factors.

First seed CDs are modified with urea, and a variety of functional groups such as -NH2 and C=O/C=N are introduced into the CDs-III system, facilitating (n, π*) electronic transitions, which in turn promote ISC from singlet to triplet excited states [2,36]. This process is crucial as it increases the population of triplet excitons. Moreover, the covalent and hydrogen bonds formed between the CDs and the silica matrix help suppress non-radiative transitions in the triplet excited states, further enhancing the delayed fluorescence by reducing the energy lost through non-emissive pathways [18]. This also contributes to the enhancement of RTP, as it stabilizes the triplet states, allowing for longer-lived emissions. The combined effects of these structural modifications lead to a thermally activated mechanism that significantly boosts TADF performance. Notably, a key factor in achieving TADF is ensuring a fast RISC rate from triplet to singlet states, which must outcompete non-radiative decay and the quenching of the triplet state as illustrated in Fig. 6(b). Even though the non-radiative transitions of the triplet excited states are somewhat suppressed in CDs-III, TADF is still difficult to observe due to the rapid rate of non-radiative deactivation inherent to the CDs system. However, after treatment with KOH, the carboxyl groups on the surface of the CDs ionize, leading to the formation of multiple ionic bonds, which helps to create a rigid network structure in CDs-IV. Unlike covalent and hydrogen bonds, ionic bonds possess stronger bond energy, which more effectively suppresses non-radiative transitions in the triplet excited states [3]. As a result, the presence of these stronger ionic bonds significantly enhances the TADF emission in CDs-IV materials, as seen in Fig. 5d. This improved suppression of non-radiative processes makes the TADF process more efficient. According to Fermi's golden rule, the rate of RISC is governed by the following equation [63]

This expression reveals that a higher temperature can significantly enhance the RISC rate by providing thermal energy to help overcome the energy gap between singlet and triplet states. However, while an increase in temperature accelerates the RISC process, it also increases the non-radiative transition rates of both the triplet and singlet states. Within a specific temperature range (298 to 343 K), the number of singlet excitons generated via RISC exceeds the losses from non-radiative decay, leading to thermally enhanced delayed emission. This is why the delayed fluorescence of CDs-IV increases as the temperature rises. However, at excessively high temperatures above 373 K, non-radiative processes dominate, resulting in the reduction of delayed emission intensity. Therefore, as the temperature increases from 298 K to 373 K, the TADF intensity initially rises but eventually declines.

Considering the excellent optical properties and extraordinary stability of the CD-II, CDs-III and CDs-IV composites, we explored their potential applications in anticounterfeiting, encryption, LEDs, and QR codes. These applications demonstrate their versatility, reliable performance, and durability. First, the CDs composites were utilized for encrypting and decrypting information as shown in Fig. 7(a). A commercial polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) material was used as the interfering agent, causing the fake message “8888” to appear under 365 nm UV light. Since commercial PVP lacks afterglow emission, the actual message was decrypted after turning off the UV lamp, revealing “1006,” “1068,” “2024,” and “1006”. Next, these composites were effectively utilized for QR code encryption. As illustrated in Fig. 7(b), the composite powders displayed unclear patterns under daylight. However, under UV light, the patterns emitted a vibrant glow. Once the UV light was turned off, the multicolored codes exhibited multicolor time-dependent afterglow in various hues, enabling easy decryption. Additionally, pattern encryption was demonstrated by utilizing the distinct afterglow lifetimes of the composites as depicted in Fig. 7(c). RTP powders of CDs-II (blue), CDs-III (green), and CDs-IV (yellow) were uniformly sprinkled onto skeletonized models of flower, moon, and panda. Immediately after turning off the 365 nm UV lamp, all three models were visible. Over time, the blue panda and green moon dimmed significantly and disappeared, while the yellow flower faded gradually before disappearing completely. The emission wavelengths of the four multicolor RTP materials spanned nearly the entire visible spectrum, enabling the creation of LEDs emitting in blue, green, and yellow, as shown in Fig. 7(d). Due to the excellent solid-state luminescence properties of the three CD composites, they were used as phosphors in combination with UV chips to prepare multicolor LEDs. The photoluminescence emission spectra of all the LEDs were measured, as presented in Fig. S19. The PL emission peaks were centred at 403 nm, 487 nm, and 570 nm, corresponding to blue, green, and yellow emissions, respectively. Furthermore, the visual emission colors were confirmed by the CIE coordinates (0.20, 0.26), (0.39, 0.54), and (0.43, 0.52). All LEDs exhibited a clear afterglow when the power was switched off, demonstrating their effective and sustained luminescent properties. As afterglow materials with bimodal emissions often exhibit color changes in afterglow with temperature, photographs of the afterglow were captured at various temperatures. As anticipated, the color shifted from yellow, and orange, to dark red depending on the temperature can be clearly shown in Fig. 7e. The corresponding CIE color coordinates were (0.55, 0.43), (0.54, 0.42), (0.52, 0.41), (0.46, 0.36), (0.40, 0.32), and (0.32, 0.26), respectively.

In conclusion, CDs composites were successfully developed with panchromatic afterglow spanning 478 to 640 nm and lifetime of 1.01 to 1.82 s. Notably, CDs-IV exhibited TADF emission, by putting CDs into rigid crystal networks which results in the formation of ionic bonds. The rigid network plays a crucial role in accelerating the RISC operation between S0 and T1 states by enhancing SOC, while successfully minimizing non-radiative transitions, thus facilitating thermally enhanced TADF. Notably, the effective transfer of energy from the RTP triplet state to the singlet state of the CDs led to TADF emission near 640 nm, with an extended lifetime of 115 ms, surpassing many previously documented red TADF organic materials. The combined effects of hydrogen bonding, covalent bonding, and physical confinement suppress non-radiative losses and enhance triplet excitons stability, ensuring highly stable afterglow even under harsh conditions. The new class of CDs composites, featuring both multicolor RTP and TADF offers promising applications in areas such as anti-counterfeiting, data encryption, and LED light illumination.
